planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; common correa is one of the most variable Australian species — occurring naturally in a wide range of habitats from coastal cliffs to mountain forests. Flower color varies from pure red through red-and-green bicolor to pink, white, and yellow-green in different forms. It blooms in winter and spring, providing nectar for honeyeating birds. Several selected cultivars are available.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; common correa is the most widespread Australian Correa species — occurring naturally across the entire southeastern corner of Australia from Queensland to South Australia, in habitats from coastal cliffs and heathlands to mountain forests — and producing in winter and early spring the pendant, tubular, fuchsia-like flowers that give all the correas their common name 'native fuchsia'. The flower form is characteristically correa: a pendant, narrow, tubular or slightly flared tube with four petals that reflex (turn back) at the tip, the whole flower dangling downward from the stem like a small, pendant, delicate bell. The flower color in the wild type is typically red to green-and-red bicolor — a vivid red tube with reflexing green petal tips — but the species is exceptionally variable, with white, pink, salmon, and pure green forms occurring naturally and several selected cultivars now in the trade. The winter bloom timing provides nectar for winter-active honeyeating birds; in cultivation it serves hummingbirds. More shade-tolerant than most Australian natives, growing well in the dappled shade of light-canopy trees. One of the most adaptable and garden-worthy of all correas.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun to part shade; brown boronia is notoriously difficult to cultivate successfully outside its native coastal heathland habitat — demanding perfect drainage, cool root conditions, and the precise soil chemistry of Western Australian coastal peat-sand. Grafted plants on Correa rootstocks improve reliability. Short-lived (5–8 years) but produces intense fragrance. Worth attempting for its unparalleled scent. The flower extract has been used commercially in perfumery.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun to part shade; brown boronia is legendary among Australian garden enthusiasts for producing one of the most intensely beautiful, complex, and powerful floral fragrances of any plant in the world — a rich, warm, spicy-floral scent with fruity undertones that has been compared to a combination of roses, sweet peas, and anise, extraordinarily powerful from a single small shrub and impossible to ignore in the garden. The fragrance of Boronia megastigma is considered by perfumers and garden writers to be among the finest natural plant scents, and commercial extraction of boronia fragrance compounds has been used in perfumery for over a century. The flowers themselves are small, pendant, four-petaled, in a distinctive two-tone coloring: dark, brownish-red to mahogany on the outside (giving the common name 'brown boronia') with bright yellow-green on the inside, hanging in clusters from the fine, dark-green, needle-leaved branches in spring. The visual effect is quiet and understated — but the fragrance transforms every garden space within range. Notoriously demanding in cultivation outside its native coastal peat heaths of Western Australia, but the reward of its fragrance for those who succeed is without peer.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; white correa is the most salt-tolerant and coastal-hardy of the commonly grown correas — native to the exposed coastal cliffs and headlands of southeastern Australia where it experiences constant salt spray and wind. It blooms in autumn through winter, providing white star-shaped flowers when few other correas are in bloom. The dense, rounded habit with attractive silver-green foliage provides year-round interest.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moist, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; white correa differs from the pendant-tubular C. reflexa in producing starry, white, open, four-petaled flowers — the petals reflexing fully to create an open, flat, white star rather than a hanging tube. The flowers are produced in autumn through winter from terminal clusters on the tips of arching branches covered in attractive, oval, gray-green leaves with a slightly woolly or tomentose texture underneath. The overall effect of a plant in flower — masses of small, clean, white, four-petaled stars scattered across the dense mound of gray-green foliage — is simple and elegant. Native to the exposed, salt-sprayed coastal cliffs and headlands of southeastern Australia from New South Wales to South Australia and Tasmania, it is the most salt-tolerant and wind-resistant of all the commonly grown correas — thriving in positions that defeat most ornamental shrubs. An excellent, reliable plant for coastal gardens in zone 9 where salt spray, wind, and lean, sandy soil combine to limit the range of ornamental shrub choices. Provides winter flowers, attractive foliage, and year-round bird habitat.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic, sandy soil in full sun to part shade; small crowea is one of the most reliably flowering Australian native shrubs — blooming from late summer through winter with extraordinary prolificacy. The clear, rose-pink, five-petaled star flowers are produced almost continuously for 4–6 months on a healthy plant. Tip pruning after spring promotes dense, bushy growth. More tolerant of shading than many Australian natives.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic, sandy soil in full sun to part shade; small crowea is one of the most valuable and reliably long-blooming Australian native shrubs for the garden — producing from late summer through autumn and winter an almost continuous succession of five-petaled, clear rose-pink to deep pink, open-faced, star-shaped flowers on a compact, bushy, densely-leaved mound of narrow, aromatic, dark-green foliage. The bloom period of 4–6 months (August through February in warm climates) makes it one of the longest-blooming of all Australian native shrubs. The flowers are in the clear, clean, warm-pink to deep-rose range — not the orange-red of many Australian natives — providing a color that is unusual in winter-flowering Australian plants. The aromatic, narrow, pointed leaves (the plant is in the Rutaceae family, related to citrus and boronia) release a characteristic pleasant scent when brushed. The compact, neat, naturally rounded habit requires little pruning to maintain. Excellent in part shade — one of the few Australian shrubs that tolerates meaningful shade and continues to flower well. A consistently reliable and rewarding garden shrub for zones 8–11.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; coastal jugflower requires excellent drainage and lean, low-phosphorus soil — like all Proteaceae, phosphorus fertilizer is lethal. It is native to the coastal sand dunes and heaths of southwestern Western Australia. The primary ornamental value is the extraordinarily fine, soft, silver-gray, silky-textured foliage — the name 'sericeus' means 'silky'. The small, tubular, red flowers in spring are secondary to the foliage in ornamental value.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; coastal jugflower is grown almost exclusively for its foliage — one of the finest-textured, most silvery-soft, most ethereally beautiful of all Australian native shrub foliages. The leaves are divided into multiple, extremely fine, thread-like silver-gray segments covered in silky, lustrous, silver hairs that catch light from every angle and create an overall impression of a plant made from fine, soft, silver-gray floss — a shimmering, luminous, soft-silver cloud of extraordinary fine texture. Each leaf has multiple, feathery, thread-like lobes covered in the lustrous, silky hairs of the species name 'sericeus'. The whole shrub creates a medium-to-large mound of incomparably fine, silver-gray, soft-textured foliage that provides year-round textural and color contrast with nearby bold-leaved or dark-leaved plants. In spring it produces small, tubular, red flowers in the leaf axils — pleasant but not the primary ornamental point. Native to the coastal sand dunes and heathlands of southwestern Western Australia where its silver, reflective foliage may reduce water loss and reflect intense sunlight. Outstanding as a silver foliage specimen or contrast plant in zone 9 Mediterranean-climate gardens requiring excellent drainage and lean soil.
